name: stash
context: Temporarily shelve uncommitted changes and restore them later — save, list, pop, apply, and drop stashes
info: Git stash saves uncommitted changes (staged and unstaged) to a stack, reverting the working tree to a clean state. This is essential when you need to switch branches but aren't ready to commit, when you want to pull remote changes onto a dirty working tree, or when you need to temporarily set aside work. Stash operates as a stack (LIFO) — you push changes on and pop them off. Each stash entry records the working tree state, index state, and optionally untracked files. Agents use stash to context-switch between tasks without creating throwaway commits. See [[git/branch]] for switching branches (which often triggers stashing), [[git/commit]] for permanently saving changes instead of stashing, and [[git/worktree]] for working on multiple branches simultaneously without stashing.
body:
# Git Stash

Stash lets you save uncommitted changes without committing them, giving you a clean working tree to do something else.

## Saving to Stash

```bash
# Stash all tracked file changes (staged + unstaged)
git stash

# Stash with a descriptive message
git stash push -m "WIP: auth refactoring halfway done"

# Stash including untracked files
git stash -u
git stash push --include-untracked

# Stash including untracked AND ignored files
git stash -a
git stash push --all

# Stash only specific files
git stash push -m "stash auth changes" src/auth.js src/auth.test.js

# Stash only staged changes (keep unstaged)
git stash push --staged
```

## Restoring from Stash

```bash
# Pop the most recent stash (apply + remove from stack)
git stash pop

# Apply without removing from stack
git stash apply

# Pop/apply a specific stash
git stash pop stash@{2}
git stash apply stash@{2}

# Apply and recreate the index (staged state)
git stash apply --index
```

**pop vs apply**: `pop` removes the stash entry after applying. `apply` keeps it. Use `apply` when you want to apply the same stash to multiple branches.

## Listing and Inspecting

```bash
# List all stashes
git stash list
# stash@{0}: On feature: WIP auth refactoring
# stash@{1}: WIP on main: abc1234 last commit msg

# Show what a stash contains
git stash show           # summary (like --stat)
git stash show -p        # full diff
git stash show stash@{1} # specific stash
```

## Cleaning Up

```bash
# Drop a specific stash
git stash drop stash@{2}

# Drop the most recent stash
git stash drop

# Clear ALL stashes (irreversible!)
git stash clear
```

## Creating a Branch from Stash

```bash
# Create a new branch and apply the stash there
git stash branch new-feature-branch
git stash branch new-feature-branch stash@{2}
```

This is useful when stash conflicts with the current branch — creating a branch from the stash's original commit avoids conflicts.

## Stash Conflicts

If the working tree has changed since you stashed, applying may cause conflicts:

```bash
# Apply stash — conflicts may occur
git stash pop
# CONFLICT in src/auth.js

# Resolve conflicts, then:
git add src/auth.js
# Note: on conflict, pop doesn't remove the stash.
# Manually drop after resolving:
git stash drop
```

## Agent Best Practices

1. **Always use descriptive messages** — `git stash push -m "reason"` makes `git stash list` useful
2. **Include untracked files** — `git stash -u` ensures new files are stashed too
3. **Prefer commits over long-lived stashes** — stashes are easy to forget; use WIP commits instead for anything lasting more than a few minutes
4. **Check stash list before clearing** — `git stash list` then `git stash clear`
5. **Use stash branch for complex cases** — avoids conflicts entirely
6. **Consider [[git/worktree]]** — if you frequently stash to switch branches, worktrees may be better

sections.title: Saving changes to stash
sections.body: Use `git stash` or `git stash push` to save changes. Add `-m "message"` for a description. Add `-u` to include untracked files. Add `-a` for untracked and ignored files. Add `--staged` to stash only what's staged. Add specific paths to stash only certain files. The stash is stored as a special commit object accessible via the stash reflog.

sections.title: Restoring stashed changes
sections.body: `git stash pop` applies the top stash and removes it from the stack. `git stash apply` applies without removing. Specify `stash@{n}` to target a specific entry. Add `--index` to `apply` to also restore the staging state. If pop encounters conflicts, the stash is NOT removed — resolve conflicts then `git stash drop` manually.

sections.title: Managing the stash stack
sections.body: `git stash list` shows all entries with their index, branch, and message. `git stash show -p stash@{n}` shows the full diff. `git stash drop stash@{n}` removes one entry. `git stash clear` removes all entries (irreversible). `git stash branch <name>` creates a branch from the stash's original commit and applies the stash there.

links.context: Commit instead of stashing for permanent changes
Worktrees avoid the need to stash when switching contexts
Review stash contents with git stash show -p

related.note: WIP commits are more visible than stashes and harder to lose
Worktrees let you work on multiple branches without stashing at all
Branch switching is the most common reason to stash

scripts.name: Stash Manager
scripts.description: Save, list, and restore stashes with safety checks
scripts.inline:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -euo pipefail

ACTION="${1:?Usage: stash-manager.sh <save|pop|list|show|drop> [args...]}"
shift

case "$ACTION" in
  save)
    MSG="${1:-WIP: $(date +%Y-%m-%d_%H:%M)}"
    if git diff --quiet && git diff --cached --quiet; then
      echo "Nothing to stash — working tree is clean."
      exit 0
    fi
    git stash push -u -m "$MSG"
    echo "Stashed: $MSG"
    ;;
  pop)
    COUNT=$(git stash list | wc -l)
    if [ "$COUNT" -eq 0 ]; then
      echo "Stash is empty — nothing to pop."
      exit 0
    fi
    REF="${1:-stash@{0}}"
    echo "Popping $REF:"
    git stash show "$REF" --stat
    echo
    git stash pop "$REF"
    ;;
  list)
    COUNT=$(git stash list | wc -l)
    if [ "$COUNT" -eq 0 ]; then
      echo "Stash is empty."
    else
      echo "$COUNT stash entries:"
      git stash list
    fi
    ;;
  show)
    REF="${1:-stash@{0}}"
    git stash show -p "$REF"
    ;;
  drop)
    REF="${1:-stash@{0}}"
    echo "Dropping $REF:"
    git stash show "$REF" --stat
    git stash drop "$REF"
    ;;
  *)
    echo "Unknown action: $ACTION"
    echo "Usage: stash-manager.sh <save|pop|list|show|drop> [args...]"
    exit 1
    ;;
esac

author: dojo-community
